retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis Ureter -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The right ureter's anatomic relationships differ from those of the left. The right ureter lies near the ascending colon, cecum, and appendix, while the left is close to the descending and sigmoid colon.

retrieved_docs.title: Ureteroscopy -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The ureters course downward and medially, connecting the ipsilateral renal collecting system to the bladder and propelling urine toward the bladder through peristaltic contractions.  In adults, the ureter typically measures 22 to 30 cm in length, with variations often correlating with body height. [2] For descriptive purposes, the ureters may be divided as follows: Abdominal: Renal pelvis to the iliac vessels Pelvic: Iliac vessels to the bladder Alternatively, the ureters may be divided into 3 segments: Upper: Renal pelvis to the upper border of the sacrum Middle: Upper border to the lower border of the sacrum, along the iliac vessels Lower: Lower border of the sacrum to the bladder

retrieved_docs.title: Ureteroscopy -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Duplicated ureters originate in a kidney pole. An incomplete duplication terminates in the adjacent ipsilateral ureter, making a Y-shaped juncture at some point. Navigating this fork  in the ureter with the ureteroscope entering only the selected renal unit can sometimes be challenging. This navigation can be accomplished using an angle tip guidewire to guide entry into the selected proximal ureteral segment.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis Ureter -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: Each adult ureter has an average length of 26 cm long, though these tubes' length can range between 22 and 30 cm long. Each ureter follows an "S" shaped curve as it travels from the kidney to the bladder. The ureter is anatomically and functionally divided into 3 segments: proximal, distal, and intramural.

retrieved_docs.title: Ureteroscopy -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: In a completely duplicated system, the proximal ureteral orifice (more lateral and cephalad)  typically drains the lower renal pole moiety and tends to reflux. The distal ureteral orifice (more medial and caudal) tends to obstruct and usually drains the upper pole renal moiety. This phenomenon is known as the Weigert-Meyer law.

retrieved_docs.title: Ureteroscopy -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: In cases of complete ureteral duplication, the duplicated ureter typically terminates in the bladder but can be inserted in different locations along the urinary tract. In boys, it may terminate between the trigone and the ejaculatory duct, whereas in girls, it may insert into the vagina, leading to continuous, uncontrollable incontinence. [7] Only females can develop incontinence from the ectopic insertion of a duplicated ureter. The distal insertion point of the duplicated ureter is a potential site for calculus obstruction. The challenge of visualizing the orifice in the bladder of a duplex ureter may be overcome using various guidewires or intravenous agents that change the color of expelled urine.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis Ureter -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: Each ureter traverses the detrusor muscle layers obliquely upon entering the bladder, forming the intramural ureter before emptying into the bladder through the ureteral orifice. This transverse entry into the bladder creates an anti-reflux valvular mechanism that prevents retrograde flow from the bladder to the kidneys during detrusor contractions. [10] The ureteral orifices are about 2.5 cm apart when the bladder is empty but can stretch up to 5 cm apart when the bladder is fully distended.

retrieved_docs.title: Ureteroscopy -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: A duplicated urinary collecting system is the most common urological congenital anomaly, occurring in approximately every 1 in 125 live births. Duplication of the ureters is bilateral in 20% of cases, frequently asymptomatic, and might be complete or incomplete.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis Ureter -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The relatively large renal pelvis gradually funnels into the proximal ureter at the UPJ. The ureters travel inferiorly from the renal pelvis apices at the kidney hila, pass anterior to the psoas, and course over the pelvic brim at the common iliac artery bifurcation. From there, these muscular tubes travel along the pelvis' lateral wall and connect to the urinary bladder. The ureters' abdominal regions are retroperitoneal throughout their course. The gonadal vessels pass anterior to the ureter a third of the way to the bladder. The ureters enter the bladder on the posteroinferior lateral wall, where they fuse with the trigone. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis Ureter -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The level of the iliac bifurcation is where the ureters cross over the iliac vessels and may be found within the cleft formed by the external and internal iliac arteries. The ureters are fixed at this location. The ureters then enter to pelvic brim, entering at an acute angle. [5] [6] [7] While not anatomically constricted, longer stones may have difficulty passing the sharp angulation as the ureter plunges suddenly posteriorly into the pelvis. It is also the point above which rigid ureteroscopy is usually discouraged. The ureter is fixed at this position, so it provides one of the few known locations where the ureter can always be found during open surgery. The UVJ is where each of the ureters enters the bladder. This site has an antireflux mechanism for preventing retrograde urine flow from the bladder to the ureter and kidneys.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis Ureter -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The proximal ureter (abdominal or upper ureter) is the segment extending from the renal pelvis to the iliac bifurcation. This region is buried mostly within the retroperitoneal fat, except where it passes directly over the psoas muscle. Thus, the proximal ureter can be hard to identify surgically. The genitofemoral nerve lies on the surface of the psoas muscle, just posterior to the proximal ureter. The ureters typically course inferior to the gonadal vessels (testicular or ovarian vessels). This surgically important relationship is best remembered by the phrase "water under the bridge," referring to the ureter's water content and uterine artery's horizontal orientation in this region (see Image . Female Reproductive System Blood Supply).

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis Ureter -- Physiologic Variants -- Ureteral or Kidney Duplication
retrieved_docs.content: In partial ureteral duplication, the proximal ureteral segments are separate but combine distally to form a single ureter before entering the bladder. In complete ureteral duplication, ureters from the same kidney enter the bladder separately. The upper renal moiety drains into the more distal, medial, and inferior ureteral orifice. Meanwhile, the lower renal moiety empties into the more superior and lateral orifice. This pattern is known as the Meyer-Weigert rule. The medial, inferior orifice is obstructed in such cases, while the lateral, proximal ureter is reflux-prone due to its shortened intramural segment. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis Ureter -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The ureters are bilateral thin tubular structures with a 3 to 4 mm diameter that connect the kidneys to the urinary bladder (see Image . Posterior Thoracolumbar Surface Anatomy). These muscular tubes transport urine from the renal pelvis to the bladder. The ureter's muscular layers are responsible for the peristaltic activity that moves urine from the kidneys to the bladder.

retrieved_docs.title: Ureteroscopy -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The muscularis layer of the ureter was traditionally thought to consist of three layers—two longitudinal layers separated by a circular layer. However, more recent studies indicate a spiral arrangement of muscle fibers that generate the peristalsis. Muscle fibers along the ureter terminate over the bladder's detrusor muscles to form the trigone. [5] The muscularis layer gradually increases in thickness from the kidney to the bladder. Consequently, complete perforations occur at a higher frequency in the proximal ureter compared to the distal ureter. [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis Ureter -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: In men, the ureters pass posterior to the vas deferens but anterior to the seminal vesicles before entering the bladder (see Image . Median Sagittal Section of the Male Pelvis). In women, the ureter passes inferior to the insertion of the infundibulopelvic ligament in the pelvis. From there, the ureter travels posterior to the ovaries and fallopian tubes. The ureter lies about 2.5 cm posterior to the uterine artery. The female ureter accompanies the uterine artery and inferior hypogastric nerve plexus in the cardinal ligament. The ureters pass close to the cervix before traveling to the bladder.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 203
retrieved_docs.content: Ureteral catheterization is a central procedural category that encompasses a range of endoscopic and imaging‑guided interventions on the ureter and adjacent urinary structures. It includes cystoscopic catheterization of the ureter, which is a specific type within the broader parent group “Cystoscopy and other transurethral procedures,” and is related to the child procedure “Transurethral excision; drainage; or removal of urinary obstruction.” The higher‑level set “Procedures on Ureter” contains ureteral catheterization and the same transurethral excision procedure, indicating their close connection. Derived interventions from ureteral catheterization comprise ureteroscopic dilatation of the ureter and bilateral or unilateral endoscopic ureteric catheterization performed under fluoroscopic guidance; the latter are also mapped from “Other x‑ray of the urinary system.” Anatomically, the ureter serves as an indirect procedure site for ureteral catheterization, while the renal pelvis

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 707
retrieved_docs.content: Ureteroplication is a surgical procedure performed directly on the ureter, targeting this tubular structure to correct or modify its function. Similarly, renal endoscopy conducted through an established nephrostomy or pyelostomy—accompanied by ureteral catheterization and dilation of the ureter—also involves direct manipulation of the ureter. Both interventions are urologic techniques that focus on the ureter, highlighting its central role in procedures aimed at diagnosing, treating, or managing ureteral abnormalities and related renal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2402
retrieved_docs.content: Ureteroplication is a surgical procedure that is performed directly on the ureter, targeting the ureter itself to correct or modify its structure for therapeutic purposes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 245
retrieved_docs.content: Ureteral catheterization is a core urologic intervention classified under Procedures on Ureter and includes specific techniques such as Cystoscopic catheterization of ureter, Ureteroscopic dilatation of ureter, and Endoscopic ureteric catheterization with fluoroscopic imaging of the upper urinary tract (both bilateral and unilateral). These endoscopic catheterizations are also mapped to Other x‑ray of the urinary system because they rely on fluoroscopic guidance. The parent category Cystoscopy and other transurethral procedures encompasses ureteral catheterization and contains the child procedure Transurethral excision, drainage, or removal of urinary obstruction, which is simultaneously a type of Procedures on Ureter, illustrating its dual diagnostic‑therapeutic role. URETER: INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS is a child of ureteral catheterization and of عملية في الحالب (Arabic “procedure in the ureter”), reinforcing its placement within ureter‑focused interventions. Kidney and pelvis

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1954
retrieved_docs.content: Endoscopic ureteric catheterization with fluoroscopic imaging of the upper urinary tract, whether performed bilaterally or unilaterally, is classified as an “other x‑ray of the urinary system.” This categorization indicates that both the bilateral and unilateral endoscopic catheterization procedures use fluoroscopic guidance to visualize the upper urinary tract and are considered radiologic examinations within urinary system imaging. Recognizing these procedures as a type of urinary system x‑ray helps clinicians understand their diagnostic and therapeutic role in urologic care, guiding appropriate use of imaging resources and informing patient management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 1 -- Community 199
retrieved_docs.content: Ureteral catheterization is a core category of procedures on the ureter that includes cystoscopic catheterization of the ureter, ureteroscopic dilatation of the ureter, and endoscopic ureteric catheterization performed with fluoroscopic imaging of the upper urinary tract (both bilateral and unilateral). These sub‑procedures belong to the broader group “Cystoscopy and other transurethral procedures,” which is a child of ureteral catheterization and a parent of “Transurethral excision; drainage; or removal of urinary obstruction.” Both ureteral catheterization and transurethral excision; drainage; or removal of urinary obstruction are classified under “Procedures on Ureter,” indicating the ureter as the primary anatomical focus. The ureter serves as an indirect procedure site for ureteral catheterization, whereas ureteroplication is a direct ureteral procedure. Combined renal‑ureteral interventions such as “Renal endoscopy through established nephrostomy or pyelostomy with ureteral

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1951
retrieved_docs.content: The kidney and pelvis are closely linked to other non‑operative therapeutic procedures of the urinary tract, indicating that interventions involving these renal structures are categorized within the broader group of non‑surgical urinary tract procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 67
retrieved_docs.content: Specific child procedures include cystoscopic catheterization of the ureter, URETER: INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS, ureteroscopic dilatation of the ureter, and endoscopic ureteric catheterization with fluoroscopic imaging of the upper urinary tract (both bilateral and unilateral). The bilateral and unilateral endoscopic catheterizations are mapped from ureteral catheterization and also from “Other x‑ray of the urinary system,” reflecting their combined therapeutic and radiographic nature. An indirect_procedure_site_of relationship links ureteral catheterization to the ureter as its anatomical site. Cystoscopy and other transurethral procedures are placed under the umbrella of ureteral catheterization and contain child interventions such as transurethral excision, drainage, or removal of urinary obstruction. These transurethral interventions belong to the broader category of procedures on the urethra, of which Cystourethroscopy with internal male urethrotomy is a member. Examination of

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1955
retrieved_docs.content: Cystoscopic catheterization of ureter is identified as a specific type of ureteral catheterization, indicating that it utilizes the same fundamental technique of inserting a catheter into the ureter under cystoscopic guidance. Ureteroscopic dilatation of ureter is described as being primarily mapped from ureteral catheterization, meaning that this dilatation procedure is based on or derived from the principles and methods of ureteral catheterization. Together, these relationships highlight that both cystoscopic catheterization of ureter and ureteroscopic dilatation of ureter are closely linked to ureteral catheterization, sharing common procedural foundations and informing each other in urological practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 95
retrieved_docs.content: Bladder outlet and prostate surgery is documented in Arabic as عملية في السبيل البولي and is grouped with other general urinary‑tract procedures, illustrating multilingual coding of urological operations. Within ureteral interventions, cystoscopic catheterization of ureter is a subtype of ureteral catheterization, and both are classified under the broader category عملية في الحالب. Ureteral catheterization encompasses several child procedures, including URETER: INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS, ureteroscopic dilatation of ureter, and endoscopic ureteric catheterization with fluoroscopic imaging of the upper urinary tract (bilateral or unilateral), which are also mapped from other X‑ray imaging of the urinary system. Transurethral excision, drainage, or removal of urinary obstruction (child procedure “Transurethral excision; drainage; or removal of urinary obstruction”) is positioned as a transurethral/endoscopic technique that subsumes ureteral procedures and links to cystoscopy and other

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 269
retrieved_docs.content: of the urinary system is a direct procedure site classified as diagnostic ultrasound of the urinary tract. The category ULTRASOUND PROCEDURES ON THE URINARY AND MALE GENITAL SYSTEMS is a child of this ultrasonography and includes kidney ultrasound (فحص الكلى بالموجات فوق الصوتية), bladder ultrasound, and combined kidney and ureter ultrasound, providing diagnostic imaging of urinary tract anatomy and function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 93
retrieved_docs.content: فَغْرُ البُوْق. Collectively, the integrated knowledge of urogenital anatomy, renal and prostate pathology, the full spectrum of female reproductive disorders, laparoscopic and peritoneal surgical techniques, extracorporeal lithotripsy, sexual dysfunction categories, and targeted pharmacologic therapies illustrates a comprehensive framework for patient assessment and multidisciplinary care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 1080
retrieved_docs.content: Ureteral catheterization serves as a central procedural category for interventions involving the ureter. Specific techniques that are classified as ureteral catheterization include cystoscopic catheterization of ureter, ureteroscopic dilatation of ureter, and endoscopic ureteric catheterization with fluoroscopic imaging of the upper urinary tract performed either bilaterally or unilaterally. These procedures share the ureter as their direct anatomic site and are often documented alongside other radiographic studies such as “Other x‑ray of the urinary system,” which are mapped from the bilateral and unilateral endoscopic catheterizations. The broader hierarchy places ureteral catheterization under the parent category “Procedures on Ureter,” indicating that all ureteral catheterizations are a subset of general ureteral procedures. Conversely, “Procedures on Ureter” is also the parent of the transurethral excision, drainage, or removal of urinary obstruction, linking these

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 130
retrieved_docs.content: network of catheter‑based, endoscopic, and imaging procedures centered on the ureter, renal pelvis, and the wider urinary‑tract anatomy, underscoring the need for coordinated procedural planning and interdisciplinary collaboration in urologic patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 1 -- Community 199
retrieved_docs.content: INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS” and transurethral excision, drainage, or removal of urinary obstruction are categorized as “procedure in the ureter,” targeting the ureteral lumen while potentially affecting the renal pelvis indirectly. Imaging of the upper urinary tract is provided by intravenous urography (Urography/pyelography, with or without KUB or tomography), known clinically as the intravenous pyelogram, which is classified as a “urinary tract image.” The intravenous pyelogram visualizes the renal pelvis and the collecting system, supporting diagnosis, procedural planning, and follow‑up. Together, these hierarchical classifications, anatomical relationships, and imaging modalities delineate how ureteral and renal pelvis procedures are organized, how they interrelate, and how clinicians can select appropriate interventions and imaging strategies for the management of obstructive urologic conditions and other urinary tract disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 223
retrieved_docs.content: The community describes a hierarchical framework of ureteral and renal pelvic procedures. Ureteral catheterization is a central procedural category that encompasses endoscopic and fluoroscopic techniques such as cystoscopic catheterization of the ureter, ureteroscopic dilatation of the ureter, and endoscopic ureteric catheterization with fluoroscopic imaging of the upper urinary tract (both bilateral and unilateral). These interventions are classified under the broader groups “Cystoscopy and other transurethral procedures” and “Procedures on ureter,” and they include the child procedure “Transurethral excision, drainage, or removal of urinary obstruction.” The Arabic‑named parent procedure عملية في الحالب (procedure in the ureter) further contains sub‑procedures such as URETER: INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS.
